description: Full-graph teaching session aimed at WRAP_UP. One solid (or intentional vague/wrong) turn per major node so nextAdvanceTarget goes null and policy wraps.
turns: Congestion control exists so senders don't flood the network until router buffers overflow and everything collapses.
The congestion window is a sender-side limit on how much unacknowledged data can be in flight, and it moves up or down based on ACKs and loss.
In slow start the window grows by about one MSS per ACK, which roughly doubles it every round-trip time.
ssthresh is the slow-start threshold — once cwnd hits it, TCP leaves slow start and grows much more carefully.
Congestion avoidance increases the window by roughly one MSS per RTT instead of doubling.
Loss is the congestion signal: either a retransmission timeout or three duplicate ACKs.
Three duplicate ACKs trigger fast retransmit — the sender resends that segment immediately without waiting for the RTO.
After a timeout, TCP Tahoe collapses the window and restarts slow start from one segment.
Fast recovery halves the window after triple-dup-ACK loss but keeps sending from the new window instead of falling all the way back to slow start.
Reno uses fast retransmit plus fast recovery so a triple-dup-ACK loss is less brutal than a timeout.
A retransmission timeout means the sender got no timely ACK, so it treats that as severe congestion and backs off hard.
AIMD means additive increase when things are fine and multiplicative decrease — usually halving — when congestion is detected.
With SACK the receiver reports every segment that arrived, not just the cumulative ack point, so the sender retransmits only the holes.
ECN lets routers mark packets when they're getting congested instead of dropping them, and endpoints cut their rate before a loss happens.
So overall: grow carefully, cut hard on loss, use dup-ACKs and timers to notice trouble, and SACK/ECN make recovery smarter when the path supports them.
I think that covers it — anything still confusing, or are we good?
